summary: Closing the Loop: What to do After a Design Critique Ends (6 minute read) Design critiques often fail because teams skip the crucial follow-up step after the feedback session ends. Without proper follow-up, stakeholders stop engaging meaningfully because they don't see their input making a difference.

comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-29, Reading time: 3 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fsimonwillison.net%2F2026%2FMay%2F28%2Fclaude-opus-4-8%2F%3Futm_source=tldrnewsletter/1/0100019e734297c0-a1b70c4a-1d24-4370-87a6-d4e30d866461-000000/1Zno9bUy5XXseiUPODvrRoDeWIFvcWjAgeykldZ__A4=452
summary: Claude Opus 4.8: “a modest but tangible improvement” (3 minute read) Claude Opus 4.8's most prominent improvement is its ability to be honest. It is more likely than previous models to flag uncertainties about its work and less likely to make unsupported claims. The model has a new feature that allows mid-conversation system messages. This means users can append updated instructions later in long-running conversations without restating the full system prompt.

title: How 2004 RuneScape fit a multiplayer RPG into 56k dial-up
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-29, Reading time: 28 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fjkm.dev%2Fposts%2Fhow-2004-runescape-fit-a-multiplayer-rpg-into-56k-dialup%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019e736c723c-82b17337-0c93-437d-8f1a-96037b321b35-000000/UssDHaSlGMzmNLgEi1UuqroLcdaCmKikn684unj-DyI=452
summary: How 2004 RuneScape fit a multiplayer RPG into 56k dial-up (28 minute read) RuneScape successfully ran a massive multiplayer RPG on 56k dial-up by meticulously minimizing the bandwidth required for every player action. This efficiency was achieved using a custom protocol where the client and server shared an identical world model, allowing the system to transmit only minimal changes, sometimes as little as a single bit for stationary players, via techniques like bit-packing.

summary: I Didn't Become a Developer to Review AI Slop (11 minute read) AI made it cheap for anyone to open a pull request, but didn't make that code trustworthy, so devs are now buried in reviewing “mystery diffs” that look fine on the surface, but may hide bugs and tech debt. summary: The Fallacy of "Defensibility" (3 minute read) The defensibility question can become a trap at seed. Most great companies do not start with a perfect moat. title: The Security Policy Rollout Survival Guide
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-29, Reading time: 4 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Foblique.security%2Fblog%2Fpolicy-rollout%2F%3Futm_source=tldrinfosec/1/0100019e73d7ff47-4d002451-e68a-42d9-8b3a-87148f87da67-000000/ftN8ABKZgfowmKyOL2aZjFow1xxFIGS5P9Lrn05pUos=452
summary: The Security Policy Rollout Survival Guide (4 minute read) When rolling out new security policies, teams should begin by communicating the details of the change, when it is going into effect, and why it is being made. Before rolling out the change, teams should run a pilot with a diverse set of users and iterate upon feedback from the pilot. Finally, when rolling out the policy teams should make enforcement of the policy visible, be in charge of enforcing it, and prepare for exceptions.

summary: Reading Observability Tools? That's a Robot's Job (5 minute read) Metrics, logs, and traces have long been the three pillars that were touted for observability tooling. comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-29, Reading time: 1 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Flinks.tldrnewsletter.com%2Fe6mxjM/1/0100019e73e4b778-da414a4d-c70a-4446-bc63-0ee92c5b000c-000000/dtsaq0pauQgQis01RitPNWtX18nM8YWTzuPXV-7IM9I=452
summary: For over a decade, we've accepted that end-to-end backprop is the only way to train deep networks (1 minute read) Holding the entire network in memory at once is why AI training is hitting a resource wall. This slashes the memory needed to train deep models.

summary: SilverTorch: Index as Model — A New Retrieval Paradigm for Recommendation Systems (7 minute read) SilverTorch is Meta's new retrieval system for recommendation engines like feeds and Reels. summary: Backpressure is all you need (32 minute read) Backpressure is the mechanism by which a downstream component signals upstream that it can't accept more work. Producers generate work when there's no backpressure and slow down, buffer, or shed load when backpressure increases. This post discusses how this concept can be used to build more ways for agents to validate more of their own work before a human has to step in. This results in longer unattended sessions that are safe enough to be useful ...

title: You Must Fix Your Asserts
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-06-01, Reading time: 12 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fkristoff.it%2Fblog%2Ffix-your-asserts%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019e82e15d45-75854c00-1cd8-4f04-bc82-64b23b16f9c1-000000/96oKif3mJAxT77kSZDblmyjC8z1KKbXIhTHB0zHpnhA=452
summary: You Must Fix Your Asserts (12 minute read) Assertions define mandatory conditions for runtime safety and compiler optimization, but disabling these checks in production is a flawed approach that allows software to run under false assumptions and masks underlying logic errors. As a result, deep domain expertise in specific industries is now the most impactful professional advantage, as this knowledge allows for verifying complex logic that generalist engineers might otherwise not be able to validate.

summary: ChatGPhish Vulnerability Turns ChatGPT Web Summaries Into a Phishing Surface (4 minute read) ChatGPT's summary renderer relies on Markdown links and image URLs from recently processed pages, which attackers can exploit to plant malicious payloads, trigger image fetches that reveal IP addresses, User-Agent, and Referer details, and display clickable phishing links, fake security alerts, or QR codes within the assistant interface.
